name: Holographic principle ('t Hooft / Susskind)
summary: Proposes that the degrees of freedom inside any closed region of spacetime are bounded by the area of its boundary in Planck units (the Bekenstein bound), so all physical information in a (d+1)-dimensional region can be encoded on its d-dimensional boundary. Formulated quantitatively by 't Hooft (1993) and Susskind (1995); given concrete realisation by AdS/CFT (Maldacena 1997). A foundational principle constraining any consistent theory of quantum gravity.

addresses_puzzles.note: If the bulk physics is encoded on the boundary, BH information is necessarily preserved — it's stored on the horizon's 2D area. Foundational for AdS/CFT and the modern resolution of the information paradox; not by itself a microscopic resolution.
The Cohen-Kaplan-Nelson holographic dark-energy bound (1999) is one application; deeper implications for Lambda are encoded in the de Sitter entropy bound and remain open.

introduces.note: A meta-principle constraining the structure of consistent quantum gravity, not a specific Lagrangian or set of fields.

history: 't Hooft (1993), 'Dimensional reduction in quantum gravity', arXiv:gr-qc/9310026 — first quantitative formulation. Susskind (1995), 'The world as a hologram', J. Math. Phys. 36, 6377, arXiv:hep-th/9409089 — popularised and refined. Maldacena (1997) gave concrete realisation in AdS/CFT. Now a load-bearing principle in modern QG.

references.citation: Susskind, 'The world as a hologram', J. Math. Phys. 36, 6377 (1995)
